text.title: best-practices-2026 Failure Modes
text.summary: Six antipatterns around adopting and operating 2026 best practices.

antipattern.description: Auto-applying AI diffs to auth, payments, or data-access code without human review.
antipattern.reason: AI tools have a measurable false-confidence rate on security-sensitive surfaces. Auto-accept turns AI assistance into uncontrolled production change.

antipattern.description: Using Copilot for every task (including 10-file refactors) OR using Claude Code for every task (including single-line completions).
antipattern.reason: Tool/task mismatch wastes tokens, IDE latency, and developer attention. The four-task partition (inline / multi-file / refactor / agentic) exists precisely because no single tool wins all four.

antipattern.description: Project still cites a `best-practices-2024` snapshot a year after it was extracted; rules reference deprecated APIs (e.g., `getServerSideProps`, `typing.List`).
antipattern.reason: Best-practice snapshots age. Without a quarterly drift scan, the constitution drifts into folklore and new developers cite obsolete rules in PR reviews.

antipattern.description: Tracked prompts in commit history that read "fix this", "make it better", "use best practices" — without context / task / requirements / output.
antipattern.reason: Unstructured prompts produce generic code that ignores project conventions. The four-part structure costs almost nothing and dramatically reduces revision cycles.

antipattern.description: `strict: true` enabled but `noUncheckedIndexedAccess` / `exactOptionalPropertyTypes` opted out via `// @ts-expect-error`.
antipattern.reason: Plain `strict` misses array-OOB and excess-property edge cases — exactly the categories of bug TS5's stricter flags exist to catch. Partial adoption leaves the gap and creates inconsistent enforcement across files.

antipattern.description: AI-drafted tests are committed without manual review against the production code they claim to verify; CI passes; the test author is also the test reviewer.
antipattern.reason: AI tests often encode the implementation, not the contract — passing even when the implementation is wrong. Author-and-reviewer-same defeats the cross-check. Mutation testing surfaces the gap; require a second reviewer for AI-drafted tests on critical modules.
